title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 05

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which federal conservation and resource policies changed conflicts over land and natural resources in the American West from 1870 to 1920.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Original simulated editorial from a mining and railroad trade newspaper, Denver, Colorado, 1878; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Newspaper-style excerpt.
“The West will not prosper if timber, coal, silver, and grazing lands are locked away by distant officials. Railroads, mines, and towns require access to the resources that make settlement possible. Eastern reformers praise scenery, but western workers know that mountains and forests must feed families as well as inspire tourists.”
Sourcing Cues: The editorial reflects the viewpoint of business interests and western boosters who favored resource extraction and local economic development.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Described federal land map created for this practice exam, western United States, 1891–1916.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows expanding areas of federally reserved forests in the Rocky Mountains, Pacific Northwest, California, Arizona, and New Mexico. It also marks several national parks, including Yellowstone, Yosemite, Mount Rainier, and Mesa Verde, as well as major reclamation projects along western rivers. A note explains that federal land reservations increased sharply after the Forest Reserve Act and Progressive Era conservation policies.
Sourcing Cues: The map emphasizes the growing federal role in managing forests, parks, water, and public lands.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement by a federal forestry official, Washington, D.C., 1906; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Government policy statement.
“Conservation does not mean refusing use. It means planned use. Forests should supply timber, protect watersheds, prevent destructive fires, and serve the public over generations. Private companies may profit from public resources, but only under rules that prevent waste and monopoly.”
Sourcing Cues: The statement reflects Progressive Era conservation ideas emphasizing expertise, efficiency, public interest, and regulated use rather than complete preservation.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Original simulated petition from small ranchers and sheep growers, Idaho, 1908; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Petition-style excerpt.
“New grazing permits require fees and rules that large outfits can manage more easily than small herders. We do not oppose protecting grass or water, but regulations written far from our valleys can decide who survives. Public land has long been our pasture, and access should not be sold to those with the strongest political friends.”
Sourcing Cues: The petition reflects tensions between federal regulation, local custom, small producers, and larger commercial users.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Original simulated essay by a preservationist club member, San Francisco, California, 1913; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Reform essay excerpt.
“A valley carved by ancient ice and filled with forests should not be judged only by the water it can deliver to a city. If every beautiful place must prove its usefulness in pipes, dams, or profits, then the nation will discover too late that it has traded inheritance for convenience.”
Sourcing Cues: The essay reflects preservationist criticism of development projects and emphasizes scenic, spiritual, and national value in protected landscapes.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Reclamation data table created for this practice exam, western United States, 1902–1918.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Federal Reclamation Trends.”
Period | Reclamation projects authorized | Main stated goals | Common conflicts
1902–1906 | several early irrigation projects | support family farms, manage water, promote settlement | costs, water rights, engineering delays
1907–1912 | projects expanded across arid western states | increase agricultural production and stabilize settlement | unequal access to water, debt, local disputes
1913–1918 | continued expansion and review | improve efficiency and repayment | conflict between small farmers, cities, and large landholders
Sourcing Cues: The table shows how federal water policy connected conservation, settlement, agriculture, and disputes over access.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Original simulated testimony from a Pueblo community representative to a regional land hearing, New Mexico, 1915; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Primary testimony excerpt.
“Officials speak of forests, irrigation, and public benefit, but our fields depend on water used by our village before these surveys were drawn. When grazing rules, dams, or outside claims change the streams, they change our ceremonies, crops, and responsibilities to one another. Public land policy should not erase older communities.”
Sourcing Cues: The testimony reflects the perspective of a Native community whose land and water use could be affected by conservation, reclamation, and federal land policies.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which federal conservation and resource policies changed conflicts over land and natural resources in the American West from 1870 to 1920. The thesis must make an argument, such as that federal policy changed conflicts by expanding national authority and expert management while intensifying disputes among businesses, small producers, preservationists, cities, farmers, and Native communities.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as westward expansion, railroad development, mining, ranching, industrialization, Progressive reform, closing of the frontier, urban water demand, public land policy, or federal support for western settlement.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about extraction, federal land management, conservation, preservation, grazing, irrigation, local conflict, or Native land and water claims.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as Yellowstone National Park, Yosemite, Forest Reserve Act, U.S. Forest Service, Gifford Pinchot, John Muir, Hetch Hetchy controversy, Newlands Reclamation Act, National Park Service, Homestead Act, Desert Land Act, or Theodore Roosevelt’s conservation policies.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze business newspapers’ interest in extraction, federal foresters’ Progressive faith in expert management, small ranchers’ concerns about regulation, preservationists’ scenic and spiritual values, or Pueblo testimony defending older land and water relationships.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as distinguishing conservation from preservation, explaining that federal policies both protected resources and enabled development, showing how “public interest” meant different things to different groups, or recognizing that western resource conflicts involved class, region, environment, and sovereignty.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that federal conservation and resource policies significantly changed western land conflicts by expanding federal authority over forests, parks, grazing, and irrigation. However, it should also show that these policies did not simply stop development. Conservation often promoted regulated use, while preservationists, businesses, small producers, cities, farmers, and Native communities disagreed over what public land should serve. Useful document groupings include extraction and western development, federal expert management, local producers, preservationist values, reclamation and irrigation, and Native community concerns. Relevant outside evidence could include the Forest Reserve Act, U.S. Forest Service, Pinchot, Muir, Hetch Hetchy, Newlands Reclamation Act, National Park Service, or Roosevelt-era conservation.
